replies.content:
1) Here you go. The passage below is the specific text from the Dolmen Arch; you'll need two gods, one older and one younger, and two goddesses, one older and one younger. (For example, Zeus and Apollo, and Demeter and Persephone.)

*******
Once you have chosen the powers you will invoke, stand with your feet parallel to one another, with the length of one of your feet between them. Divide your weight evenly between your feet, and bend your knees very slightly. Keep your spine upright but not stiff, as though you were suspended from the top of your head.
Standing in this position, you will make the same gestures as in the Christian sign of the Cross, but with different words, images, and intentions. Perform the ritual as follows:

1. Touch your forehead, and say the name of the elder of the two gods, or the first of the four powers you’ve chosen to invoke: for example “Hu the Mighty, great Druid god.” As you do this, imagine that the sun is far above your head, radiating golden light downwards toward you. See a ray of golden light descending to the crown of your head and passing within, to a point at the center of your head, where it forms a sphere of brilliant light a few inches across. Feel the warmth of the light radiating outwards through your head.

2. Draw your hand down to your solar plexus, the space just below where the two sides of your ribcage separate. Say the name of the younger god, or the second power: for example, “Hesus of the Oaks, chief of tree spirits.” Meanwhile, imagine that the ray of golden light descends further, creating a second sphere of brilliant light at your solar plexus. From there, as you invoke your second power, the ray extends straight down through the midline of your body into the ground beneath you, extending all the way down to the heart of the Earth. Visualize this as a brilliant sphere of silvery-green light, the color of sunlight on moving water, as large and bright as the sun but located far below you.

3. Bring your hand up to heart level, and then out to your right shoulder, and say the name of the elder goddess, or the third power: for example, “Ceridwen the Wise, keeper of the cauldron.” Imagine a second ray streaming out to the right from the sphere of light at your solar plexus, flowing through your right side in a straight line out to infinite distance. Hold this image as you invoke your third power.

4. Draw your hand across to your left shoulder, and say the name of the younger goddess, or the fourth power: for example, “Niwalen of the Flowers, child of spring.” Imagine a third ray streaming out to the left from the sphere of light at your solar plexus, flowing through your left side and across the palm of your left hand in a straight line out to infinite distance. Hold this image as you invoke your fourth power.

5. Cross your hands over your chest, right over left, and say, “May the powers of nature bless and protect me, now and always.” Here imagine two more rays of light streaming out from the sphere at your solar plexus. One of them goes straight ahead of you into infinite distance, the other straight out behind you into infinite distance. At this point the sphere of light in your solar plexus is the meeting place of six rays of light extending above, below, to your left, to your right, ahead of you, and behind you. Hold this image as you say the final invocation.

I seem to have no trouble recalling the feel and smell, I do a decent job with visualizing the image of the Buddha on the cover, the Bantam logo on the spine, and even a sort-of-ok job with the bar code on the back, but the text is giving me fits. You've mentioned in the past that you had trouble with visualization. Is it normal to have extra difficulty with text? Do you have any tips for strengthening that skill in particular, such as maybe focusing on each letter, then on pairs of letter, and build up to the whole word?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's entirely normal, because the brain tries to switch from visualizing to reading when it sees text. When you read, you don't notice the shapes of the letters -- you simply take in the meaning of the word at a glance, and that's done in a different part of the brain from the visual cortex.

For now, you might want to leave the text alone; alternatively, look at it until you can see it as a shape rather than as a word, and try that.

replies.content:
If I may share my experience and perspective:

As a child I was afraid of the dark in an immense way. Luckily, I had a sense very specifically of what I was afraid of: I was terrified that my psychic contents would become real, that in the dark there would be no distinction between my imagination and actuality and that I would become the prey to mental processes that I couldn't control.

While this is rather dramatic, I think that perhaps this accounts why people are afraid of the dark, and what they fear.

Think of the number of US Citizens who spend their day at a soul numbing job, spend their free time getting high, and watching television and generally trying not to think. Many people take psychiatric medication, in part, from my perspective as a layperson, to gain the upper hand on their own psychic processes, to gain an upper hand on their soul.

Indeed, the whole present moment awareness meditation fad concerned stopping thinking, quieting the mind, and ignoring thoughts, feelings etc.

And the lights go out and there is no noise and people are left alone with their thoughts, thoughts which they cannot control, thoughts that possess them and whisper the unmentionable truths of their situation directly into a resisting awareness. They understand that life is a fragile thing, that they are uncomfortable with the choices they are making, that their soul is dissatisfied with their life, that the civilization is dying, etc etc.

And so people hide from every sort of darkness; silence is a darkness of sound, and so people fill their lives with every noisy device blaring music and talking at them, they have the cell phone to constantly distract from quiet, the huge amount of flooding lights, screens filled with flooding light.

Carl Jung wrote something to the effect that "people will do anything, no matter how absurd, to hide from their souls." Given the rather dire psychic environment in the United States this dictum is, I think, taken to the nth degree.

replies.content:
There seems to be an ancient strong instinct in people to avoid any place that might give a predator some concealment. This instinct probably is a product of evolution, just as birds whon have never encountered a hawk themselves or seen what one can do, still give an alarm call whenever they spot one.

For people who rely primarily on sight to assess a situation, and make relatively little use of hearing, smell, taste or their general skin-senses, darkness gives concealment to any actual or imaginal predator. That, of course, is most people in our modewrn world.

This has something to do, too, with whether a building feels "off" in some way, or even haunted. Can one easily (and unconsciously) map the layout of the building in one's mind so that it clearly has no spaces for anything to hide from one's view. Or is the layout full of odd angles and hidden corners, even actual spaces to which there is no access? (Cf. Lovecraft's "Dreams in the Witch House.") When I was teaching magic at Brown, I was occasionally asked by one or another of my students to take a look at their apartment and do something about the presumed "spook." In every case but one, it clearly had to do with the building's utterly bizarre layout in the place that felt haunted, and dealing with the student's terror-instinct was enough to fix things for the student. (Yes, some ritual helped in the process.) There was even one such apartment that two students, many years apart, asked me to look at.

And outside in nature, does the immediate environment itself seem unfamiliar enough that one has no idea what sort of unknown predator might live there? Same result. (Cf. Machen's "Children of the Pool.") In Machen's story, the protagonist's instinct of terror follows him out of the the environment in question and back to his everyday life; nor does it leave him until a knowledgeable friend figures out what is going on, checks out some of the details, and sees a way to dispell the terror that has trapped the protagonist.

comments.title: Changeling Issue
comments.content:
My grandfather developed schizophrenia and spent the second half of his life in a hospital sedated for decades. He claimed my father (his biological son) was a changeling. I believe it caused my father to experience alienation and psychological harm. I have read several descriptions of this fairytale creature and understand that the label could historically even lead to the death of the person on the receiving end.
Would this issue be similar to being cursed? Would a strong enough SOP ritual be enough to banish the effects of this labelling from my family even if my father lives in another county? How would I incorporate this notion into an SOP or would the ritual banish any dark entities associated with it automatically? Would a better remedy to simply ignore it and not focus? Or perhaps build on the other side of the idea and perceive it literally as my grandfather did but look at it as a positive and be happy to be part of the fairy world...

replies.title: Re: Changeling Issue
replies.content: Unless you have any reason to think that your father actually was a changeling, the best thing to do is to treat it as the ravings of a lunatic and ignore it.

replies.title: Re: Changeling Issue
replies.content:
It sounds like your grandfather developed Capgras delusion, the delusion "that a friend, spouse, parent, or other close family member (or pet) has been replaced by an identical impostor."

One modern take I've read on changelings is that the designation of "changeling" provided a justification in a Christian society for the practice of exposure for children who would be burdensome.

Unlike a lot of modern non-explanations of folklore, this one seems to actually explain the phenomenon, including why it seems to have been exclusive to Europe.

replies.title: Divination in magical system learning
replies.content:
Dear JMG,

I am currently looking at two divinatory systems to start using in druidry training. Tarot and Astrology.
What would in your opinion be the advantages of one or the other?

replies.title: Re: Divination in magical system learning
replies.content: Tarot is much quicker to learn and many people find that it gives crisp, accurate predictions even when you're just getting started. (That was certainly my experience.) Astrology is much more complex, and it'll take a couple of years of close study before you can use it to answer the kind of questions a Tarot reader with a month or two of diligent practice can answer with ease. All in all, I'd recommend Tarot first, so you've got a good flexible oracle to hand, and then get started on astrological study.

replies.title: Re: Divination in magical system learning
replies.content:
I learned Tarot divination from the B.O.T.A. deck and the Waite/Smith deck (both of which were influenced by the Tarot deck developed by the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn) in a college dorm with one simple book, Eden Gray's The Tarot Revealed as my instructor. As a young woman away from home for the first time, I had plenty of questions to ask, and I agree with JMG that it doesn't take a long time to learn enough Tarot to be useful.

For people who take in information visually, the Waite/Smith deck and many of the more recent decks that are variants of it have an additional feature that more mathematical systems like the I Ching, astrology and western geomancy lack. These decks have very detailed illustrations. The details and the colors they are printed in are not random, nor are they solely chosen by the artist's personal taste. They are derived from a correspondence system based on the Kabbalistic Tree of Life.

Whether or not you have a specific interest in Kabbalah, the mental habit of taking a limited set of categories and assigning a color, an element, an animal, a letter of the alphabet, a number, a plant, a direction, a magical tool, and so forth to each category creates a web of mental associations that can be used in multiple ways. Once you have absorbed a correspondence system of some kind (not necessarily one based on Kabbalah), you can use it for divination, as a memory aid, to design spells and rituals, to learn herbalism, and as a way of communicating messages through art.

From ancient times up until the end of the Renaissance, everybody had a mental stock of correspondences, and learned people had a bigger stock than most. Most people believed or assumed that the correspondences were based in material and spiritual reality, rather than being arbitrary human inventions. The revolution in the methods of scientific inquiry that took place in the early modern period wound up tossing out astrology, the Doctrine of Signatures, the four elements, and all the rest, because these strings of associations turned out to be less useful than analytical experiment for finding out the answers to a lot of questions in chemistry, physics, astronomy and biology.

This entire way of thinking got chucked out as superstitious hooey. We aren't brought up to think sytematically about associations. As a result, we overlook or fail to understand a lot of information encoded in prescientific visual art and have a hard time following the trains of thought in older books about magic and spirituality. Studying Tarot is a painless way of learning how to think in terms of associations and correspondences.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Huh. Is there a particular reason why not? In your writings you say divination is one of the core practices of magic, so it seems interesting that many systems wouldn't include it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Remember that the magical systems of the Western world are still trying to recover from many centuries of suppression and persecution. In the twilight years of the classical world, Christian clerics and mobs motivated by ignorant fear and prejudice destroyed vast amounts of irreplaceable knowledge and abolished magical traditions that had thrived for thousands of years before then; while later generations of clerics picked up some of what survived and kept it safe, the damage had already been done. Then, centuries later, the great magical resurgence of the Renaissance was followed by the scientific revolution on the one hand and a hardening of religious orthodoxies on the other, and a great deal that had been recovered during the Renaissance was lost or neglected. The modern magical revival only really got going in the 1850s; we're still piecing together fragments of half-forgotten lore, and figuring out how to do things that the mages of the past did with ease.

The use of divination as an essential element of magical training was introduced in modern times by the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, which required its initiates to practice geomancy, Tarot, and astrology. That turned out to work very well, and after the Golden Dawn papers were published by Israel Regardie just before the Second World War, that practice spread through much of the occult community. Go back much before that, though, and you'll find it only in groups that were directly influenced by the Golden Dawn.

comments.title: Born in a shirt

does it have any significance if somebody is born either „with a caul“ or „en caul“? I looked up there is a difference between these two. In Czech we say „be born in a shirt“.

Hana

replies.title: Re: Born in a shirt
replies.content: In many European countries it's a bit of traditional lore that a child who's born with a caul -- that is, with a piece of the amniotic membrane on his or her head -- will have unusual psychic gifts. I don't know that anyone's done a study to see how accurate the tradition is, but it's probably worth paying attention to.

replies.title: Re: Born in a shirt
replies.content:
I believe that in many sea faring or fishing communities a caul was believed to confer immunity to drowning. It did not have to be the person's own caul--in at least some groups it was considered worth purchasing a caul from a landlubber.

Being born in a caul is less common these days because physicians routine rupture the membranes if labor is prolonged.

comments.title: Mindfulness craze
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

A lot of big companies have been endorsing the practice of mindfulness meditation in their workplaces in recent years. It has become a mainstream habit in the middle class. Considering the side effects of Eastern types of meditation (like dulling the abstract thinking abilities), do you think that this craze is motivated by some sinister goal, like making these people into only task-oriented working machines who do not question anything? What could be the reasons behind this fashion?

replies.title: Re: Mindfulness craze
replies.content: As someone from an upper class background, there's nothing nearly so sinister. Rather, a bunch of them have realized that mindfulness meditation dull abstract thinking abilities, and when the privileges you can't stand to go without depend on the immiseration of the majority of the world's population, anything which makes that easier to ignore is going to be popular.

replies.title: Re: Mindfulness craze
replies.content: As someone from an immiserated lower working class background what you describe a 'nothing nearly so sinister' sounds even more sinister.

replies.title: Re: Mindfulness craze
replies.content: As with most fads these days, there are many motivations at work in the mindfulness fad. A lot of people in the comfortable classes recognize at some level that their lives are empty due to a lack of contact with spiritual realities, and mindfulness meditation as marketed in the industrial world seems to offer a way to fill the void without having to question any of the conventional wisdom of our time. A lot of people in the comfortable classes are also looking for any way to run away from their own thoughts, and especially from the unavoidable but unmentionable awareness (as noted by the commenter above) that their pretensions to being the "good people" fit very poorly with the fact that their privileges depend on driving most of the world's population into poverty and misery. Corporations are glad to further this because people who don't think too deeply make better cogs in the corporate machine. So it's a mixed bag, as usual.

replies.title: Re: Mindfulness craze
replies.content:
The vibe I got when it was presented at a company meeting was that it was being used as a stopgap for people feeling stressed/burned out. I didn't see it as a way to avoid questioning things -- except maybe workloads -- so much as a temporary inoculation against stress. After all, it saves the company from having to bother to try to fix the underlying problems, at least for a little while longer. Other companies might be using it other ways, though.

Likewise, we offer a budgeting course -- Dave Ramsey or something similar, I don't remember the specifics -- to employees, which the cynic in me sees a way to avoid actually paying people more.

(And yes, of course there are there individuals who could benefit from mindfulness meditation.)

comments.title: possible psychic attack
comments.content:
I have been working with a person who has been extremely vindictive with me for the past couple months ever since I defied their unreasonable demands. Lately they have been instructing others to pass me up for opportunities in order to teach me a lesson. Bad enough, right?

There has been a pattern of our conflicts immediately followed by my illnesses, which I had considered a result of stress on my system. On Wed I filed a public notice regarding the issues in question that others have complained about as well, and within 45 minutes I got a terrible back spasm that ruined the holiday weekend.

It has been years since I have had a spasm, and never this bad. Usually they ease up on the next day but this one persisted until day 3 when out of desperation I tried the sword and torch method out of the appropriately titled DF book that had been sitting on my shelf (half-read) for years.

On the first couple days I tried SOP and got some relief, but had to do it twice a day to cover both sun up and sun down. On the third day I was also bloated and vomiting in addition to the spasms. Using meditation I followed a line up my spine and out to a spot just below and to the right of my solar plexus. I found the connection and imagined who I thought the source was, I put up the glass barrier then hacked, slashed, and cauterized the nub. Immediately I felt relief and my mobility improved.

Today was much better, and I am actually able to walk without a cane and did some laundry. Tomorrow I have an appointment with a masseuse who also has energy worker training and let them know in advance what I believe to be the cause.

Besides timing, my suspicions regarding this other person are based on a few other reasons. They had previously brought up the subject of spirituality in an attempt to get me to admit to what it is that I believe in, but I did not reveal anything specific besides a general admission of higher forces at work. When our disagreement intensified they used Jedi mind trick-style hypnosis techniques to undermine my self confidence, and when that didn't work they moved on to shameless gaslighting. My impression is that because the SOP results were limited this was likely not a conscious attack, but I do not have much experience and per instructions from the book am avoiding occult practices (like divination) until this is over. The techniques I did try were out of pure desperation as it was a holiday weekend and nobody was available to help.

One thing I picked up from the book is that DF does not like cannabis very much, and I have been using edibles a lot lately to help me sleep which I also stopped after the 3rd day. My justification was that my use was not to the point where I was hallucinating, but apparently it still leaves the wrong doors open for an attack to sneak through.

I know you cannot comment on the medical aspects, but hopefully you can give me some advice on how to improve my defenses when it comes to coping with the other realms. The past few months have really done a number on me and according to my astrology report the roller coaster does not stop until after next summer.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: possible psychic attack
replies.content: I'd strongly encourage you to consider using the hoodoo protective bath and the red bag amulet discussed in the Magic Monday FAQ, as those offer good protection against this sort of thing. Yes, this sounds like a classic form of psychic attack, and the fact that the person involved asked you about your spirituality to try to figure out how to carry out the attack suggests to me that it's deliberate and intentional. Quite a few people know how to do that sort of thing these days -- the more corrupt end of the New Age movement spread such instructions fairly widely, and of course there are also unethical Neopagan and occult teachings that do the same thing.

comments.title: Something to do with magic.
comments.content:
Hi John
Several questions, some of which have something to do with magic.
First, I’ve started reading The Cosmic Doctrine in order to play along with your study guide. The last time I tried to read it, it was absolute gibberish. How come it makes sense this time? Like I got around to needing a philosophy, so now I can develop one?
Second, speaking of philosophy, are you getting any mileage out of Derrida’s hauntology? Nostalgia for the future you didn’t get certainly suggests an explanation for some of the phony outrage of various fashionable people over assorted fashionable causes… or might it be a symptom rather than a syndrome?
Complete aside – Hauntology has lent its name to a musical subgenre. Wizardren unfamiliar may find some entertainment value in “There Only Once” by the Soundcarriers.
And third, what is there to be gained by building symbols into a floor (or walls or ceiling)? Tile, parquet, what have you. Is this better than chalk or a carpet? If you don’t mind commenting on fictional magic, what might such a floor do in your Weird of Hali RPG?
Many thanks.
Rusty

replies.title: Re: Something to do with magic.
replies.content:
1) That's fairly common. The first time I tried to make sense of the Cos. Doc. I got precisely nowhere; I think that encountering the metaphors, letting them percolate through the subconscious mind, and then returning to the text may be helpful.

2) My encounters with Derrida's ideas in the early 1990s, during my second pass through college, were sufficiently off-putting that I've basically ignored him ever since, and in fact I've tended to steer clear of any French philosophy more recent than Sartre and the Situationists. The concept of "hauntology" sounds worth a look, though. Can you point me to a not too opaque source?

3) If you put something like that permanently into a structure you have the option of consecrating it the way you would consecrate a talisman, and thus dramatically increasing its effect. You can't do that with chalk. You can do it with a carpet, which is where the "flying carpet" of Middle Eastern legend comes from -- the "flying" is astral, of course.

3a) Whatever the GM wants it to do. It could make conjuration easier by providing the conjurer with a protective circle he or she doesn't have to cast; it could provide additional Voorish Points for sorcery, or help replenish spent Voorish Points the way a standing stone or moon path does; it could have a spell of any kind worked into it, which could be triggered by a PC speaking a certain word or simply by standing on the right bit of floor, and so on. That would be a very fun thing to put into one of those abandoned houses that feature so largely in August Derleth stories!

replies.title: Re: Something to do with magic.
replies.content: Re (2), I'm a copyeditor of academic books, and the term "hauntology" is coming up fairly often, although I'm having trouble pinning down a specific source that would be useful. I gather Derrida meant it in a pretty abstract way, but recent authors seem to be making it rather more literal. I've recently edited a couple books whose authors take notions of animism and haunting quite seriously, discussing things like river spirits without hedging at all.

replies.title: Re: DMH Tool Enchantments
replies.content:
People who are in the early stages of taking up witchcraft and who share living space with people who are unsympathetic sometimes have similar issues. I'm interpreting your question as a concern about privacy, not about where to store your equipment.

If worry about being observed or interrupted is your concern, one workaround might be to do your enchantments and ceremonies elsewhere. Perhaps you can find an outdoor spot nearby that other people are not going to be watching or walking through. This would of course require dressing for the weather, but the gear you have to carry is pretty portable.

If going out is not practical, and you want to do the gestures and calls without drawing human attention. you may need to choose times when the rest of the household is asleep or elsewhere. I recommend doing your work in an area of the dwelling that the other people do not spend a lot of time in, definitely not in a shared bedroom. Even if you are meticulous about closing down the grove, the energy of the space may change if you work in it regularly. Some people are sensitive to energy in spaces without knowing why. The energy may attract them or repel them; it's hard to predict. There are ways to clear a space completely after use, but then you don't have the benefit of the residual energy yourself.

comments.title: Pathworking
comments.content: With tarot and the Tree it's obvious where to start with pathworking but what about those using a Hellenic system? What would the natural progression be?

replies.title: Re: Pathworking
replies.content:
Why not do what a lot of people have done, and map the Hellenic gods onto the Tree? As Gershom Scholem's great work on the origins of the Cabala points out, the Cabala was not originally Jewish -- the Jews got it from the Gnostics, and a good case can be made that the Gnostics got it from Greek Neopythagoreans, who developed it out of the tetractys. (Read John Dillon's book on the Middle Platonists and you'll be astonished to find how many basic Cabalistic ideas came straight out of Platonism.) That's why so many Cabalistic terms (for example, sephirah and gematria) are borrowed from Greek.

It would be the easiest thing in the world to (re)construct a Hellenic Tree of Life. Simply translate the names of the spheres into Greek -- Kether becomes Stephanos, for example, Chokmah becomes Sophia, Binah becomes Noesis, and so on -- and assign Hellenic gods and goddesses to the ten spheres -- Sallust did most of the work already in On the Gods and the World -- and you're good to go. If you need the advice of someone who's been up and down the Tree a few times, just ask. ;-)

replies.content: Do you know if anyone's ever done something like this in the direction of making the Tree more Christian/Catholic, by converting the Sephirah and mapping them to attributes/epithets of God derived from Christian theology? I know there's been interweaving between the Jewish and Christian strains of Qabalah throughout their history, but I'm curious if anyone's tried to revise the system from the ground up to bring it in line with later theological developments.

comments.title: Questions and answers tossed out to the universe
comments.content:
I am a visual artist. I have two distinct bodies of work that are related but visually quite different. I alternate between the two, doing one for several months then back to the other. I do this because of my personal interests and exhibition/sales demands. While working on one set of paintings recently with the intent to do so until spring, I suddenly had the strong sense that I should be working on the other type of artwork as soon as I finished up my current painting. I kind of tossed off a comment while working – “Okay universe, should I start working on the other?” By the end of the day through what seemed like random events such as an unexpected email correspondence, a magazine image and an unsolicited comment from a friend, I gathered that the answer was a very loud Yes! I am comfortable taking those as specific signs meant to answer my question, and will proceed accordingly.

My questions is this – In general, if one asks a question out into the universe like I did, and then encounters situations that would seem to provide answers, are those answers reliable and are they safe? replies.title: Re: Hoodoo Bath question
replies.content: After. Shower to get yourself physically clean, and then take the hoodoo bath. replies.title: Re: ENM
replies.content:
Glamour might also work.

Invisibility spells are easy enough to do with visualization. Just put a cloud of fuzz and attention deflection around the car. I use them once in awhile when parking in a sketchy neighborhood or an illegal parking spot. Major drawbacks, though. You might not be able to find your own car in a shopping mall lot, and if you forget to take the spell off, it's dangerous.

A big protective field of "Don't mess with me, you'll get caught," might work. I've never tried that on a car, but when I was a carless young woman I used it a similar glamour on myself whenever I hit the street.

For everyday magic, I like glamour, because the less powerful glamour spells require nothing but a moment of focused attention and visualization. They are dead easy to refresh, and if not refreshed, will fade in potency on their own (in my experience), because they are just a fog which has not changed the inner structure of the thing itself. Glamour is a form of mental manipulation, but the sorts of glamour I attempt are merely ways of attracting or turning away someone's attention for a little while, and so not very different from mundane actions (putting on perfume and cosmetics) that most people accept as ethical.

comments.content:
Hey John,

James from Hermitix here,

After our recent discussion I began thinking about Occultism in general in relation to Kantian philosophy. Excuse the long comment here, but I need to articulate myself well I think, so:

To posit time and space as a priori, they are absolutely – always already – necessary for there to be anything at all. Such a placement wherein time is prior to space is not accidental. For there to be perception of material there must be space, but for there to be space it must exist within time; time is always primary to space. This overly simplistic articulation of the transcendental aesthetic from Kant’s The Critique of Pure Reason allows for the following conclusions regarding the aforementioned theorizations in relation to man. Man must exist within time and space, along with the entire cosmos, but man, due to his very nature can only attend to/perceive reality via his processor – his brain. As such, the way in which he perceives is a matter of synthetic process, the forms of time and space he senses are not pure, they are synthesized/processed versions of spatio-temporality particular to the output of man’s senses. What man perceives is a representation of the real, he synthesizes both temporal and spatial reality and in doing so his perception, he, creates his reality as he represents it.

From Kantian critique we can thus make a clear split - Two separate terms referring to the spatio-temporal synthesis of man (his reality), and the form of time and space external to these syntheses. The former synthesis from man is henceforth called the ‘Inside’, and the a priori spatio-temporality (and later, production) which is external to this ‘Inside’ is called the ‘Outside’.

If we take this philosophy/theorization as given, then can Occultism be simply seen as purely transcendental, in that, rituals, practice and revealing-the-hidden is simply a matter of communicating with the Outside?

Or do you think that even Kantian assumptions regarding the a priori already assume too much in relation to man's knowledge of the cosmos?

Further note: Schopenhauerian philosophy is entirely compatible with Occultism, and that's simply a continuation of Kantian critique with some theology stripped away and pessimism piled on-top, so I can't see why Occultism could be viewed through transcendental philosophy...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In some mystical experiences, the mystic will perceive a reality that encompasses all things as a single thing, quite unconstrained by the dimensions of time and space. This single thing includes everything that has existed, does exist or will exist in time and space, and much more that has never entered the realm constrained by time and space. Moreover, it is neither alive nor not alive. These perceptions, when they come, do not come through any of the bodily senses, and thus presumably could come to a person all of whose bodily senses have been rendered inactive. Yet these same perceptions (unlike, say, mathematical proofs) are not intellectual perceptions, either, but something wholly other than either sensory or intellectual perceptions.

If such experiences do occur, and if they are self-consistent (as they seem to be) among many experiencing people, then they would seem incompatible with your postulates.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'd be amazed if occultism couldn't be viewed through transcendental philosophy. Every philosophy (as distinct from ideology) should be able to account for magic, just as it should be able to account for agriculture, navigation, Mexican cooking, and Bach concertos played by an orchestra of kazoos: the point of philosophy (at least as I understand it) is to provide a basic mental framework within which all human experience can have a place, and if it fails to do that it fails as a philosophy. (Cough, cough, the intellectual bankruptcy of logical positivism, cough, cough.)

As for Schopenhauer, while he certainly takes Kant as one of his starting points and Hindu philosophy for the other, I don't think it's fair to say that his work is simply a continuation of Kant's critique. He's arguing that reality is fundamentally nonrational, and that reason is merely a grade of the Will and is thus useful rather than true; if I understand correctly, that chops away the entire basis of Kant's critique.

replies.title: ritual garments
replies.content:
What JMG wrote applies to both solitary and group work, and I think is the primary benefit.

As with any other kind of social group, when ritual garments are worn by a group meeting together, it reinforces the sense of membership in the group. If the garments (or lack of same) are identical, the intent and often the effect is to create a sense of social equality for the duration of the meeting. Conversely, if there are distinctions in dress, they reinforce whatever social hierarchy and differentiation are norms in the group.

Identical loose robes with deep hoods that obscure the face render the wearers anonymous, especially when the meeting is lit only by a few candles. Being freed of one's everyday social identity allows other parts of the personality to come forward.

Ritual garments or jewelry may be imbued with protective power. They may also incorporate visual symbols which are reminders of the group's beliefs. The number and colors of the fringes on a Jewish prayer shawl are examples of this, as are many ecclesiastical garments.

replies.title: Re: ritual garments
replies.content: Right. That's explicitly why the Charge of the Goddess specifies working skyclad. Note: the origins of that part of it was Leland Godfrey's "Aradia," which is Italian. Skyclad does not work in colder climes unless the participants wear a coat of grease, so many traditions work robed as you described. British Witchcraft certainly does.

replies.title: Re: ritual garments
replies.content:
Been doing this a while in covens based in four different traditions. One met in street clothes, one skyclad for coven meetings and variously costumed for public rituals, and two are related traditions of British origin, one of which met skyclad as a ritual requirement and the other robed, both always indoors. The only thing I haven't experienced is masked ritual.

I've had some experience doing skyclad outdoor rituals, mostly in warm weather, which is delightful.

If meeting skyclad outdoors in cold weather, either everyone needs to be healthy and fit, or the ritual must include a lot of lively dancing and a minimum of standing around listening to people orate, or include consuming a fair amount of alcohol, or wearing a coat of grease as you say (I've tried this once with grease from the Yule goose) or with a big bonfire in the middle, or some combination. It's possible but in colder climates unlikely to be a routine practice.

Meeting skyclad indoors, my experience has been that once things get going, we warm up. This is partly from exercise if there is lively dancing, but partly seems to be magical power raised by the group. If it's a dud ritual, I'll feel cold.

replies.content:
I went through a lot of sleep paralysis episodes when I was younger (and wouldn't wish it on anyone!). Invoking the name of Jesus was pretty much the only thing that helped.

I do not wish to give offense, and hope you will not read me that way (I'm Christian: not trying to proselytize!), but it is my understanding that one of the bigger reasons that Christianity got off to such a rollicking start in the ancient Roman world (which was not deficient in gods!) was that its adherents were such formidable exorcists. There's even that fun passage in Mark 9 where the disciples are griping because people who aren't part of "the club" are casting out demons in Jesus' name, and Jesus is like "Dude, leave them alone: if they're not against us, they're FOR us!" (paraphrase!)

So... while I can't say anything about your relationship with your deity, at the very least, Jesus doesn't seem to mind being invoked for that purpose.
